summary: gate() and explain() each re-read-and-reparse lock.toml several times per invocation via source_deps' own independent parse — hoist to the already-parsed declarations document

per.section: Cost scale is hoisted, and pinned by count

edit.description: `source_deps` (2472) does its own `fs::read_to_string` + `.parse::<DocumentMut>()` of `lock.toml`, independent of `read_declarations`'s (3398) own full parse via `declarations_from_doc` (3439, which already takes a parsed `&DocumentMut` — the doc-scoped-extractor shape DRIFT-EMIT-LOCK-PARSE-HOIST establishes for the provenance side, upstream in this chain). `source_deps` is called by `layout_imports` (2497), `includes` (2508), and — via `source_dep_stale` (2522) — by `layout_import_stale` (2563) and `include_stale` (2580): four independent re-reads/re-parses on top of `read_declarations`'s own. Within one `gate()` invocation (src/main.rs) this lands as 5 full parses of the same file in one run: `read_declarations` (main.rs:858), `layout_imports`+`includes` via `import_edges_from_lock` (main.rs:891), then `layout_import_stale`+`include_stale` (main.rs:1208-1209) — each a fresh read+parse; `explain()` (main.rs:513, 525, 587) repeats the same pattern minus the two staleness calls. `Declarations` (struct 2668) does not currently carry the `layout_import`/`include` lock families read back from disk — `includes: Vec<IncludeRow>` (2691) is documented seam-inbound-only, 'never written back into this declaration table, so a lock round-trip reads it empty' — which is why `source_deps` re-parses independently rather than filtering an already-parsed set. Fix: extend `declarations_from_doc` (or a parallel doc-scoped extractor, the same pattern DRIFT-EMIT-LOCK-PARSE-HOIST establishes) to lift the `[declaration.layout_import]`/`[declaration.include]` families from the one already-parsed document, and have `layout_imports`/`includes`/`source_dep_stale` filter that in-memory set instead of each independently opening and parsing the file. Land after EXTRACT-FOUNDATION-BOUNDARY-RESTORE, the last entry in the existing chain to touch this file, so this entry's extractor sits beside the settled post-move shape rather than racing it.

`gate()` (847) and `explain()` (513) each call `drift::read_declarations` once and then separately call `import_edges_from_lock` (2563, calling `drift::layout_imports` then `drift::includes`) — two more independent parses on top of the first — and `gate()` additionally calls `drift::layout_import_stale`/`drift::include_stale` (1208-1209), two more still. Once `source_deps`'s callees read off the shared parsed document instead, thread the already-parsed document/`Declarations` value through these call sites rather than re-deriving it — the same one-parse-per-run shape `read_declarations`'s own call already establishes for the rest of the declaration family.

summary: gate() recomputes the edge-resolution walk 2-4x per invocation — check's own copy plus resolved_edges/resolved_arcs re-derived by acyclic/degree/mention_reachable — consolidate to one shared walk

per.section: Cost scale is hoisted, and pinned by count

edit.description: `check` (pub fn, 111-129) is a from-scratch copy of the admissible-edge x source x target walk, producing dangling-route `Diagnostic`s. `resolved_edges` (pub(crate), 967-990) is the identical iteration shape producing `Vec<ResolvedEdge>`; `resolved_arcs` (private, 1001-1010) folds a fresh `resolved_edges` call (1006) into adjacency. `acyclic` (195-211) calls `resolved_arcs` at 196 (always-on); `degree` (236-...) calls it again at 252 (opt-in); `mention_reachable` (355-...) calls `resolved_edges` directly at 371 (opt-in) — so one `gate()` invocation recomputes the same walk 2 (check + acyclic, always-on) to 4 times (adding degree/mention_reachable when their selections opt in). Change `resolved_arcs` to fold an already-computed `&[ResolvedEdge]` slice into adjacency instead of calling `resolved_edges` itself (drop the call at 1006); change `acyclic`, `degree`, and `mention_reachable`'s signatures to accept the shared `&[ResolvedEdge]` (computed once by `gate()`) rather than `edges`/`by_kind`, folding/filtering over it. `check` needs the *unresolved* half `resolved_edges` discards (a dangling route is exactly what it skips) — the real design decision, left as a judgment call for build (name the choice in the commit body): either (a) widen `resolved_edges` to return both the resolved set and the dangling occurrences in one pass (a `(Vec<ResolvedEdge>, Vec<Diagnostic>)` or small struct), retiring `check`'s separate loop in favor of reading its half off the shared pass, or (b) give `check` its own filter over a richer per-target enumeration `resolved_edges` derives from, keeping it a thin projection rather than a parallel walk. Whichever shape lands, reword `resolved_edges`'s doc comment (964-966) and the module header's READ-EDGE-UNIFY claim (11-13, 'one resolved-edge enumeration... shared') to state the runtime-sharing this consolidation establishes, not just the type-sharing it already had.

`gate()` (847) calls `graph::check` (1131, always-on), `graph::acyclic` (1147, always-on), `graph::degree` (1154, opt-in), and `graph::mention_reachable` (1162, opt-in) — four independent triggers of graph.rs's walk today. Once graph.rs's functions take the shared `&[ResolvedEdge]` slice (or the widened resolved+dangling shape), compute it once here, after `graph::admissibility` (1130) and before the four consumers, and thread it into each call, replacing the `edges`/`by_kind` pair those calls pass today wherever the walk itself moved to the caller.

`why`'s narration (`resolved` at 335) calls `graph::resolved_edges(edges, by_kind)` directly — a separate verb invocation from `gate()`'s own run, so it is not part of the within-gate duplication this entry fixes and needs no consolidation of its own. It is `resolved_edges`'s only consumer outside graph.rs (doc-comment cites at 21, 62, 309, 470) — if the design decision above widens `resolved_edges`'s return shape, this call site's usage narrows to the resolved half; verify at implementation time whether the signature change ripples here, and update the call plus the four doc-comment cites to match whatever shape lands.

existing acyclic/degree/mention_reachable/check inline tests pass calling the consolidated functions over the shared slice — signature-only change, no diagnostic output changes

acceptance: gate() computes the admissible-edge x source x target walk exactly once per invocation; acyclic, degree, and mention_reachable derive their adjacency/edge set from that one shared computation instead of each independently recomputing via resolved_edges/resolved_arcs, and check's dangling-route diagnostics derive from the same shared pass. A count-pin test proves it. No behavior change (engineering.md, 'Cost scale is hoisted and pinned by count'). `cargo test` and `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` green.

per.section: Cost scale is hoisted, and pinned by count

edit.description: `resolve_kind_units` (fn 1319, doc comment 1302-1318: 'the one corpus both gate and explain range over') walks a kind's governs locus and reads+parses every member file off disk — whole-input work scaling with the harness's member count. `gate()` (847) and `explain()` (513) each invoke it TWICE per kind, per run: once through the two-greens dispatch — `gate()`'s built-in loop (917-945) calls `kind_features` (1518-1529, which calls `resolve_kind_units` at 1524) per built-in kind at 938, and its custom-kind loop (974-989) calls it per custom kind at 982; `explain()` reaches the same built-in path via `builtin_features_by_kind` (1843-1853, calling `kind_features` per kind) at line 532, and inlines a direct `resolve_kind_units` call for custom kinds at 556 — and a second time through `collect_directive_members` (2166-2197, called by `gate()` at 1004 and `explain()` at 599), which independently re-derives `builtin_kind::definitions()` (2171, already held as `builtin_defs` in both callers), re-calls `resolve_kind_units` per built-in kind (2173) and per custom kind (2186), and re-runs `partition_kind_rows` (2183, already computed at gate():955/explain():549) to get the same custom-kind rows a second time. Every member document in the corpus is read from disk and parsed twice per `gate()`/`explain()` invocation. Secondary, same functions: `gate()` also calls `builtin_kind::definitions()` a second time inline at ~1193 as an argument to `coverage_note::check`, discarding the already-computed `builtin_defs` (cheap, in-memory, but fixable alongside). Fix: thread the units/features `kind_features`'s callers already resolve into `collect_directive_members` instead of a second `resolve_kind_units` pass — the exact carrying shape (widening `kind_features`'s return to keep each unit's `source_path` beside its `Features`, or having callers hold the `Vec<Unit>` alongside) is a judgment call for build; name the choice in the commit body.

summary: InstallEntry.placement is four bare &str consts matched with a wildcard fallback in gate_installed — the MODELINE case is never named, only reached via `_`

per.section: A shared concept is one type

edit.description: `InstallEntry.placement: &'static str` (370) is one of four consts — `SESSION_START` (91), `MODELINE` (93), `GUARD_HOOK` (95), `NOTE` (97) — set at its five construction sites (582, 606, 612, 642, 656). `gate_installed`'s tally (522-533) matches them as `SESSION_START => hook = true, GUARD_HOOK => guard = true, NOTE => notes += 1, _ => modelines += 1` — `MODELINE` is never named in the match, reached only through the `_` fallback, which would also silently absorb any future fifth placement as a modeline, uncaught by the compiler. This is the shared-concept posture's own described failure shape ('a `_` arm over a shared concept is a seam defect waiting for its field report'). Replace the four string consts and the `&'static str` field with a real `#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)] pub enum Placement { SessionStart, Modeline, GuardHook, Note }`, update the five construction sites and `gate_installed`'s match to name every variant exhaustively (no `_` arm), and give `Placement` a `Display`/label method carrying today's exact strings ('session-start hook', 'schema modeline', 'guard hook', 'managed-by note') for the two `.to_string()` uses at 540/543 and the report line built from it.

`outcome_of` (137-147) and `has_entry` (150-152) take `placement: &str` and compare with `==` against `entry.placement`; ~15 call sites pass string literals (`"session-start hook"` 233/430/544, `"guard hook"` 236/428/717/751/897/918, `"managed-by note"` 237, `"schema modeline"` 238/440/790). Once `InstallEntry.placement` becomes `Placement`, both helpers' signatures change to `placement: Placement` and every call site's string literal becomes the matching enum variant (`Placement::SessionStart`, `Placement::GuardHook`, `Placement::Note`, `Placement::Modeline`).

tests.asserts: every existing outcome_of/has_entry assertion (hook/guard/note/modeline placements across the install/re-install/dry-run coverage) passes unchanged calling the enum-typed helpers — the change is representation-only, no behavior change to which placements land or their reported outcome

acceptance: InstallEntry.placement is a real Placement enum with an exhaustive match in gate_installed (no `_` arm); the four bare &str consts are gone. cargo test and cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings green.

Change the signature to `requireSections = (sections: readonly string[]): Predicate => ({ key: "require_sections", sections })`. Add a `sections?: readonly string[]` field to the `Predicate` interface (76, beside `section`), documented the same way `keys`/`values` are ('`require_sections`'s required heading list').

`clauseRow` (65-115) maps each `Predicate` field to its `ClauseRow` column by explicit `predicate.key === "..."` branches (`keys`, `values`, `section`, etc. — no generic spread). Add: `sections: predicate.key === "require_sections" && predicate.sections ? [...predicate.sections] : undefined,` alongside the existing `keys`/`values`/`section` branches (110-114), copying into a fresh array per the file's own documented reason ('the generated rows carry mutable columns; the predicate's ... is read-only, so copy each into a fresh array').

`ClauseRow` (struct at 2869-2971) carries no column a `require_sections` row could use — `keys`/`values`/`section` are documented as each other predicate's own. Add a new field `pub sections: Option<Vec<String>>,` after `section` (2970), with a doc comment naming it `require_sections`'s required-heading-list column, matching the `#[serde(default)]` pattern every other optional column carries. `predicate_from_row` (424-...) has arms for every predicate key except `require_sections` and the constructor-less `dependency-exists` (falls through `_ => return None`). Add, near the `must_define` (458) / `section_contains` (461) arms: `"require_sections" => Predicate::RequireSections { sections: row.sections.clone()? },`.

summary: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` cites a memory doc that now reads four hops: the gate constant and its own source disagree, and the doc sentence cannot settle which counting is temper's

gate.reason: A human probe rules the hop semantics — the doc text cannot. Fetched live at 420da04 (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-16): 'Imported files can recursively import other files, with a maximum depth of four hops.' Both readings survive that sentence: the root's own import is hop 1 and the chain caps at four edges (⇒ 4), or 'imported files ... recursively import' names a recursion four deep ATOP that first import (⇒ 5, today's constant). Only measuring the runtime's actual load depth decides it, the headless-testbed act that produced the `(mention-gate-containment)` evidence — never plan's to guess and never build's to fetch, because guessing here edits a live gate's semantics: a cap too low forges a dead-member finding on a member that loads, a cap too high suppresses a real one (intent.md invariant 2).

edit.description: The constant and the cite that justifies it. `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` (59) sits under a doc (55-58) asserting 'the `at-import` grammar is recursion-capped at five hops (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-02)' — a number the live page does not say. temper's own indexing IS decidable on disk and needs no ruling: `live_members` (630) seeds every own-live member and runs `for _ in 0..MAX_IMPORT_HOPS` BFS rounds (657), one import edge per round, so the constant counts EDGES traversed from a live seed. What no reading of the disk settles is which edges the doc's 'four hops' counts — hence the park. Whatever the ruling: the cite is re-retrieved, re-dated, and re-worded to state the hop-counting it encodes, never left asserting a bare number its source does not carry. The two narration cites deferring to it — 528 ('hop-capped at [`MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`]') and 626-629 ('capped at [`MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`] hops (the `at-import` recursion depth the format documents)') — carry the same claim and move with it, as does the propagation comment at 652-655 ('capped at the format's import recursion depth'). 6667265 (mention-reachable, in the b85df4a..HEAD window) shifted every address after ~519 — the const (59) and its doc (55-58) sit above the edit and are unmoved; the later addresses re-read at HEAD, so every address carries.

`the_import_closure_is_hop_capped` (1407) already pins the boundary mechanically rather than in a comment — `r1..r5` rescued from a live seed, `r6` dead as the sole finding — so it encodes today's 5 in the one place a flip cannot silently miss, and it is the test that moves if the ruling is four. Its comment (1408-1412) spells 'capped at the format's five-hop import recursion' (1410) and carries the same claim as the constant's cite; re-word it to the ruled reading rather than leaving the fixture narrating the retired number. 6667265 (mention-reachable, in the b85df4a..HEAD window) added the mention-reachable fixture and shifted this test down; addresses re-read at HEAD, so they carry.

tests.asserts: the import closure rescues a chain exactly as deep as the ruled cap and no deeper — the boundary hop pinned at the ruled number, in the ruled counting

acceptance: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`, its doc cite, and the two narration cites that defer to it all agree with the live docs, and each states which hop-counting it encodes (spec-system.md, 'Form rules': an external fact carries its source and retrieval date where it is enforced, verified against current docs, never encoded from memory). The boundary hop stays pinned by a test at the ruled number. `cargo test` green.
notes: Park re-tested at d1af9a5 and holds — nothing ruled the hop semantics; VERIFIER-TYPED's window touched graph.rs but not the const region (still 5 at graph.rs:59, cite still 2026-07-02 at 55-58). The park's subject is untouched, and may still be wrong. A build-side friction capture records the same disagreement; the human's to read, not plan's to drain. rescoped 4a33911.

summary: drift.rs's write_placement (mkdir+write+DriftError::Write) is reimplemented inline in emit_manifest and emit_one instead of calling the existing helper

summary: The clause algebra gains `when`: a guard predicate (enum/type) over an addressable field conditioning a body of ordinary clauses, one shared address binding, no nesting (decision 0041)

edit.description: Decision 0041 ratifies a conditional clause form: a clause may carry a **guard** — one predicate from the scalar-decidable set (initially `Predicate::Enum`/`Predicate::Type` only, both of which already carry their own `field`; the 26-variant `Predicate` enum spans 132-399) — conditioning a **body** of ordinary clauses, evaluated only at the elements the guard's field locates (shared address binding: each element `FieldPath::locate`, address.rs:135-141, yields is judged independently, body clauses evaluating at that same element), silent — no finding, not even a vacuity — wherever the guard doesn't hold, guarded-field absence included. No nesting: a body clause carrying its own guard is inadmissible. The cleanest fit given the existing architecture: add `Predicate::When { guard: Box<Predicate>, body: Vec<Clause> }` as the 27th variant — `Clause` (44-68: label/severity/predicate/guidance/source) stays structurally unchanged, matching the precedent `RequirementRow` already sets nesting `Vec<ClauseRow>` (drift.rs ~3036-3054) — but the concrete representation is build's call; name the choice in the commit body. Whichever shape, EVERY already-exhaustive match over `Predicate` in this file needs a `When` arm added at implementation time, since two of them ship exhaustive ahead of this entry in the chain (CONTRACT-DECLARED-KEYS-EXHAUSTIVE-MATCH, CONTRACT-REQUIRE-SECTIONS-ROUNDTRIP, both earlier in the blockedBy chain this entry sits behind): `target` (642-675), `documented_field` (686-722), and `declared_keys` (736-745, post-exhaustiveness-fix) all report `None` for `When` — guard/body carry no field or schema key of their own to expose the way a single-field predicate does. `predicate_from_row` (424-548, 24/26 keys handled today, falls through `_ => return None` at 546) gains a `"when"` arm decoding the guard sub-predicate and recursing over the body's nested `ClauseRow`s via `compose::clause_from_row`. Also add `Shape::LeadingDotSlash` (783-803, joining `HyphenPlacement`/`NoXmlTags`) — decision 0041's second, smaller addition, the relative-path spelling rule marketplace's `source` string form documents — updating every exhaustive match over `Shape` (`name`/`from_name`/`pattern`/`match_holds`/`admits`/`demand`, ~813-897) with the third arm.

`ClauseRow` (2878-2979) needs a shape carrying a `when` row's guard and nested body through the lock. `RequirementRow` (~3036-3054) already nests `pub clauses: Vec<ClauseRow>` for an unrelated reason (a requirement's own attached clauses) — the same nesting shape is available here: a `when` row's guard (reusing the existing `value_type`/`values` columns the way a bare `Enum`/`Type` row already would) plus a `body: Option<Vec<ClauseRow>>` column nesting the body's own rows. `stamp_clause_label` (1363, writes every clause row's compiled address over the whole lock) needs to recurse into a `when` row's nested body rows so each body clause still gets its own address. Exact column shape is build's call, matching whichever `Predicate::When` representation contract.rs lands; document it the way every other `ClauseRow` column already is. This regenerates `sdk/src/generated/ClauseRow.ts` via the existing ts-rs export test — do not hand-edit the generated file.

`clause_from_row` (238, `Result<contract::Clause, ClauseRowError>`) decodes a `ClauseRow` via `severity_from_label` (239), `contract::predicate_from_row` (243), and the row's `label` (248). Once `predicate_from_row` gains a `"when"` arm, decoding the guard predicate itself needs no structural change here — but decoding the body's nested `ClauseRow`s requires recursing into this same function per nested row, surfacing a malformed body clause as a loud `ClauseRowError` rather than dropping it silently (extend the existing `Severity`/`Predicate`/`Label` variants or add one for a malformed body row; name the choice in the commit body).

A second `clause_from_row` (25, doc 9, decoding `ClauseRow`s for the embedded built-in lock read path) needs the same `when`-decoding update as `compose::clause_from_row` — verify at implementation time whether the two functions' `when`-handling can share one body-decoding helper rather than each carrying its own copy, since this file's own header already frames itself as the mirror path `compose.rs`'s function serves.

`admissibility` (110-118) and `inadmissibilities` (153-163, per clause: `judgeless`, `bodyless`, `unaddressable`, `vacuities`) gain the two `when`-specific admissibility checks the decision names: the guard predicate is restricted to `Enum`/`Type` (any other guard predicate is inadmissible, never silently accepted), and a body clause whose own predicate is itself `When` is inadmissible (no nesting) — sited wherever build judges fits the existing per-predicate-rule shape. Since ENGINE-PREDICATE-FENCE-EXHAUSTIVE-MATCH (this entry's own blocker) makes `bodyless`, `judgeless`, and `vacuities` exhaustive matches ahead of this entry, all three also need an explicit `When` arm at implementation time (mapping to their existing fallback value — `None`/`None`/`Vec::new()` — unless the design decision above chooses to fold the no-nesting/guard-restriction check into one of them instead of a new helper). `addressed_field` (201-235, doc 198-200: 'The match names every arm rather than defaulting') gains a `When` arm returning `None`, matching the key/marker/set predicates' existing `None` arms. `decide` (774-1101, per-member exhaustive match) and `judge` (487-540, whole-selection match) both gain `When` arms implementing the shared-binding evaluation: `decide` locates every element the guard's field addresses, judges the guard at each, and for each element where it holds, evaluates every body clause's own `decide` at that same element, folding results — silent at elements where the guard doesn't hold or the field is absent.

Per engineering.md's gauntlet-corpus and vacuity bars: add a `when`-guarded clause over a mixed collection (one string-form element, one object-form element — mirroring the marketplace `plugins[].source` shape decision 0041 is driven by) proving the shared-binding invariant: the object-form element's guarded body clause fires only at that element and never leaks a finding onto the string-form element; plus a case where the guarded field is absent everywhere, asserted as the explicit vacuous-but-legitimate empty case, never silently inherited.

Every exhaustive match over `Predicate` (`target`, `documented_field`, `declared_keys`, `addressed_field`, `bodyless`, `judgeless`, `vacuities`, `decide`, `judge`) answers the new variant by construction. `cargo test` and `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` green.
